metadata.title: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I get that a spin-half state picks up a minus sign after one 360-degree turn and only returns home after 720, because theta enters as theta-over-two on the Bloch sphere, and neutron interferometry actually detects that flip through destructive interference at 360. I want to understand the topology underneath. Help me: (1) explain how SU(2) double-covers the rotation group SO(3), (2) tie the half-angle to why global phase hides the sign in any single measurement, (3) describe the belt or plate trick as a physical demonstration of the 720 return.

props.command: I get that a spin-half state picks up a minus sign after one 360-degree turn and only returns home after 720, because theta enters as theta-over-two on the Bloch sphere, and neutron interferometry actually detects that flip through destructive interference at 360. I want to understand the topology underneath. Help me: (1) explain how SU(2) double-covers the rotation group SO(3), (2) tie the half-angle to why global phase hides the sign in any single measurement, (3) describe the belt or plate trick as a physical demonstration of the 720 return.
props.segment: Why an Electron Needs Two Full

beats.narration_text: Rotate an electron's state by a full three-hundred-and-sixty degrees — one complete turn — and it comes back as the negative of itself. Only after two full turns, seven-hundred-and-twenty degrees in all, is it truly home.

card.copy: Why an Electron Needs Two Full Turns to Come Back

beats.narration_text: A normal vector, rotated three-sixty degrees, is identical to where it started. That is how rotations work — or so we think. An electron's spin state picks up a minus sign after exactly one full turn. Expectation values don't change, but the state itself does.

Neutron interferometry measured this. Send a beam of neutrons along two paths. Rotate the spins on one path by a controlled angle. At three-sixty degrees, the two beams destructively interfere — as if they are out of phase by pi. At seven-twenty degrees, they constructively interfere again. The sign flip is real.

The Question: every physical quantity you can measure is unchanged by the sign flip — probabilities square the amplitude. Nothing observable changes. But the sign flip is still real — interferometry detects it. How can something both invisible and real at the same time?

card.copy: 360-degree rotation flips the sign. Probabilities don't change. The flip is invisible — but interferometry detects it. How?

beats.narration_text: Naive reading: a rotation by three-sixty should return any physical state to itself — that is the definition of a full rotation. The minus sign cannot be physical if every measurement outcome is the same. It must be a mathematical artifact with no meaning.

The mechanism: every spin-half state lives on the Bloch sphere with a half-angle. The general state is cos-of-theta-over-two times spin-up, plus a phase times sin-of-theta-over-two times spin-down. The angles theta and phi locate the state on the sphere, but they appear halved inside the trigonometric functions.

Rotating the physical axis by theta means theta goes to theta plus two-pi. The half-angle then goes from theta-over-two to theta-over-two plus pi. Cosine of theta-over-two plus pi equals negative cosine of theta-over-two. The entire state flips sign. One turn, minus the state.

The sign flip is invisible on its own: the probability of any outcome is the squared modulus, so minus-psi and psi give identical probabilities. But put the flipped state into interference with an unflipped reference beam, and the minus sign shows up as a pi phase shift — destructive interference. Interferometry measures relative phases.

The implication: ordinary three-dimensional rotations form the rotation group SO(3). Spin-half states live in SU(2), which wraps around SO(3) twice. Every ordinary rotation corresponds to two spin-half operations. The physical rotation group and the spin state space are related, but not identical.

A student asks: if the sign flip is invisible to all measurements, why does it matter? The answer: it matters the moment you compare two states. Interfere a rotated neutron with an unrotated one. At three-sixty degrees of rotation on one path, the interference minimum appears — exactly the zero you expect from a pi phase shift. The sign was never just a bookkeeping artifact; it was waiting to be measured.

Why two full turns? The spin state carries a half-angle. A full rotation advances that half-angle by pi, flipping the sign. The flip is invisible in any single measurement — probabilities are squared. But it appears in interference as a pi phase shift. Two full turns bring the half-angle back, and the sign returns.

card.copy: Half-angle in state  →  360 deg flips sign.  Sign invisible alone  →  real in interference.  720 deg restores the state.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.
